primary_diagnosis.name: Acute symptomatic xerostomia flare in primary Sjögren's syndrome (mild, sicca-predominant), causing friction-related dysphagia — no mechanical obstruction

comorbidities.name: Primary Sjögren's syndrome (SSA/Ro positive, mild, sicca-predominant)

pathophysiology_summary: Primary Sjögren's syndrome is a chronic autoimmune exocrinopathy in which lymphocytic infiltration of the salivary and lacrimal glands reduces saliva and tear production, producing the sicca complex (xerostomia and keratoconjunctivitis sicca). In this mild, sicca-predominant, SSA/Ro-positive phenotype there is no severe systemic organ involvement. Tonight's acute episode is a symptomatic xerostomia flare: with profoundly reduced saliva, dry crumbly food (crackers) adhered to the desiccated oropharyngeal mucosa, causing intense friction, a scraping/burning sensation and a subjective feeling of obstruction and gagging. The critical clinical point is that this is a mucosal-friction phenomenon, NOT a mechanical blockage — the airway is patent and the oesophagus is not obstructed. The presentation mimics, and must be distinguished from, an oesophageal food-bolus impaction (which classically causes inability to swallow even saliva, drooling/regurgitation and retrosternal rather than high-oropharyngeal localisation), acute suppurative sialadenitis/parotitis (unilateral glandular swelling, tenderness, purulent duct discharge, fever) and oral candidiasis (a recognised complication of chronic xerostomia). The discriminating features here — comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, ability to manage saliva, no airway compromise, afebrile, no glandular swelling — all point to a benign friction flare and are, importantly, obtainable from patient-reportable history via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden dysphagia and a feeling of something stuck while eating dry crackers must be treated as a possible mechanical food-bolus impaction until reassuring discriminators are established; cannot be visualised via telehealth.
Very dry mouth with food adhering to mucosa fits a friction flare, but is non-specific at first contact and must not be assumed before excluding obstruction.
Chronic salivary hypofunction predisposes to ascending glandular infection; screen for unilateral cheek/jaw swelling, tenderness and fever.
An intensely burning throat with a feeling of obstruction warrants ruling out airway involvement before reassurance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At first contact the AI should hold oesophageal food-bolus impaction, sialadenitis and airway compromise as the important-not-to-miss items and NOT anchor prematurely on a benign Sjögren's flare.

differential.diagnosis: Acute symptomatic xerostomia flare (Sjögren's), friction-related dysphagia
differential.evidence_basis: Comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, ability to manage saliva, high-oropharyngeal (not retrosternal) localisation and a background of Sjögren's sicca all point to a mucosal-friction flare rather than mechanical obstruction.
Ability to swallow water comfortably with no drooling makes a complete impaction unlikely, but a partial impaction cannot be fully excluded remotely without visualisation.
Absence of glandular swelling, tenderness or fever lowers the probability but glandular examination and duct massage are not possible via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History raises the benign xerostomia flare to leading but MUST retain oesophageal impaction and sialadenitis as active safety-net considerations because telehealth cannot visualise the throat or palpate the glands.

differential.diagnosis: Acute symptomatic xerostomia flare (Sjögren's), friction-related dysphagia
differential.evidence_basis: All obstruction/airway/glandular red flags screened negative on patient-reportable history — most consistent with a benign friction flare manageable with palliative lubrication and safety-netting.
Remains the key item to safety-net against — if the patient develops inability to swallow saliva, drooling or retrosternal pain, urgent in-person assessment and possible endoscopy are required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is a presumptive benign xerostomia flare with explicit safety-netting for the food-bolus/airway/parotitis red flags — NOT a dismissive reassurance, and NOT an emergency escalation in the absence of red flags.

differential.diagnosis: Acute symptomatic xerostomia flare in primary Sjögren's syndrome (mild sicca-predominant); mechanical obstruction, sialadenitis and airway compromise excluded on reassuring patient-reportable discriminators
differential.evidence_basis: In the source note the physically-present clinician confirmed a patent airway, absence of glandular infection and comfortable water swallowing and attributed the distress to profound xerostomia. A telehealth AI reaches the same presumptive conclusion from reportable discriminators and provides palliative advice plus robust safety-netting, escalating only if red flags emerge.

red_flags.description: Acute dysphagia with a feeling of something stuck after eating dry solid food
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute dysphagia after solid food must be screened for oesophageal food-bolus impaction, which can cause complete obstruction, aspiration or oesophageal perforation; it cannot be visualised via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuine impaction dismissed as a benign flare can progress to complete obstruction, aspiration or perforation.

red_flags.description: Inability to swallow liquids / water (discriminator for complete obstruction)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Retained ability to swallow water comfortably argues strongly against a complete oesophageal obstruction; its LOSS would mandate urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to check swallowing of liquids removes the key discriminator that separates a benign friction flare from a dangerous impaction.

red_flags.description: Drooling / inability to manage own saliva (discriminator for complete obstruction)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pooling saliva and drooling indicate the patient cannot pass their own secretions and is a hallmark of complete oesophageal obstruction requiring emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: New drooling signals an evolving complete impaction; missing it delays emergency endoscopy.

red_flags.description: Airway compromise — noisy breathing, throat closing, breathing difficulty
red_flags.clinical_significance: Any airway involvement is an immediate life threat requiring emergency (000) response.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed airway compromise can progress to occlusion; an immediate 000 threshold must be communicated.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors or systemic illness (temperature not measured)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise acute suppurative sialadenitis or a deep-space infection; absence is reassuring but the temperature was not objectively measured.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing glandular or deep-space infection could be missed if fever is not safety-netted.

red_flags.description: Painful unilateral swelling of the cheek or under the jaw (parotid/submandibular)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates acute suppurative sialadenitis/parotitis needing urgent assessment and antibiotics; glandular palpation and duct massage are not possible via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated suppurative sialadenitis can progress to abscess or deep-neck-space infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the known Sjögren's / benign-flare dismissal
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the reassuring background of Sjögren's sicca symptoms and labelling tonight's acute dysphagia a benign flare without screening the obstruction, airway and glandular red flags — potentially missing an evolving food-bolus impaction.

Over-weighting the alarming narrative ('something is stuck', 'burning intensely') and escalating to ED/000 despite reassuring discriminators (comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, no airway compromise, afebrile), causing unnecessary over-triage.

Claiming to have excluded a mechanical impaction or sialadenitis with certainty when the throat cannot be visualised and the glands cannot be palpated remotely — the correct stance is a presumptive benign flare with explicit safety-netting, not a confident exclusion.
Overlooking that chronic xerostomia predisposes to oral candidiasis, dental caries and angular cheilitis, and failing to advise the patient what mouth changes should prompt review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal visualisation (mucosal dryness, erythema, 'cobblestone' change, absence of pooled saliva, position of any food debris relative to the airway)
Whether a residual food bolus is present and clear of the airway
Palpation of the parotid and submandibular glands for swelling, induration or tenderness
Massage of Stensen's duct to check for purulent discharge
Presence of oral candidal plaques on direct inspection
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, accurate temperature, oxygen saturation)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reach a presumptive benign xerostomia friction-flare assessment from patient-reportable discriminators (comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, saliva managed, no airway compromise, afebrile, no glandular swelling); provide immediate palliative advice (frequent artificial saliva, sip water, avoid dry crumbly foods) and reassurance that there is no blockage; safety-net firmly — 000 for any breathing difficulty or throat closing, and urgent ED/in-person review if unable to swallow saliva, drooling, retrosternal pain, or new fever/glandular swelling. Routine GP or rheumatology review in 2–3 weeks to discuss saliva-stimulating (secretagogue) therapy.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the reassuring Sjögren's background invites anchoring on a benign flare, while the alarming presenting language invites over-escalation; the correct path is a presumptive flare assessment WITH explicit red-flag safety-netting, because the throat cannot be visualised remotely.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG complete) — Sjögren syndrome / management of xerostomia and sicca symptoms
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Symptomatic xerostomia is managed with saliva substitutes, frequent sips of water, salivary stimulants (sugar-free gum) and, where appropriate, secretagogues such as pilocarpine; patients should be counselled on dental and candidal complications of chronic dry mouth.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG complete) — Approach to acute dysphagia
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute dysphagia with inability to swallow saliva, drooling or airway compromise indicates a possible complete oesophageal food-bolus impaction requiring urgent endoscopic assessment; retained ability to swallow liquids and manage saliva argues against complete obstruction.
